questions.text: What is the primary motivation for separating user logical memory from physical memory in a virtual memory system?

choices.label: To ensure that the entire program is loaded into memory before execution begins
choices.text: To ensure that the entire program is loaded into memory before execution begins

choices.label: To allow the logical address space to be much larger than the physical address space
choices.text: To allow the logical address space to be much larger than the physical address space

choices.label: To reduce the CPU utilization by limiting the number of concurrent programs
choices.text: To reduce the CPU utilization by limiting the number of concurrent programs

choices.label: To force the programmer to manage memory allocation manually
choices.text: To force the programmer to manage memory allocation manually

questions.explanation: Virtual memory allows the logical address space to be significantly larger than physical memory, enabling programs to run even if only partially loaded.

questions.text: Which of the following are benefits of implementing virtual memory? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Increased CPU utilization and throughput
choices.text: Increased CPU utilization and throughput

choices.label: Reduced I/O needed to load or swap programs
choices.text: Reduced I/O needed to load or swap programs

choices.label: Faster execution of user programs due to less I/O
choices.text: Faster execution of user programs due to less I/O

choices.label: Programs are constrained by the physical limits of RAM
choices.text: Programs are constrained by the physical limits of RAM

choices.label: Ability to run more programs concurrently
choices.text: Ability to run more programs concurrently

questions.explanation: Virtual memory removes physical memory constraints, allowing more programs to run, reducing unnecessary I/O, and improving system efficiency.

questions.text: In a virtual address space, why is it beneficial to have the stack and heap grow toward each other from opposite ends of the logical memory?

choices.label: To minimize the use of the page table
choices.text: To minimize the use of the page table

choices.label: To prevent the MMU from mapping logical addresses to physical frames
choices.text: To prevent the MMU from mapping logical addresses to physical frames

choices.label: To maximize address space use and enable sparse address spaces
choices.text: To maximize address space use and enable sparse address spaces

choices.label: To ensure that code is always loaded in the center of memory
choices.text: To ensure that code is always loaded in the center of memory

questions.explanation: This design creates a 'hole' in the middle of the virtual address space that can be filled as needed, allowing for dynamic growth of heap/stack and sharing of libraries.

questions.text: What is the definition of a 'lazy swapper' in the context of demand paging?

choices.label: A swapper that swaps out processes only when the CPU is idle
choices.text: A swapper that swaps out processes only when the CPU is idle

choices.label: A swapper that never swaps a page into memory unless it is specifically needed
choices.text: A swapper that never swaps a page into memory unless it is specifically needed

choices.label: A swapper that only handles kernel-level memory
choices.text: A swapper that only handles kernel-level memory

choices.label: A swapper that loads all pages of a process at once to avoid page faults
choices.text: A swapper that loads all pages of a process at once to avoid page faults

questions.explanation: A lazy swapper (or pager) only brings a page into memory when a reference is made to it, minimizing unnecessary I/O.

questions.text: If a page table entry's valid-invalid bit is set to 'i', what does this indicate during address translation?

choices.label: The page is read-only
choices.text: The page is read-only

choices.label: The page is currently in physical memory
choices.text: The page is currently in physical memory

choices.label: The page is not currently in memory (Page Fault)
choices.text: The page is not currently in memory (Page Fault)

choices.label: The page has been modified and must be written to disk
choices.text: The page has been modified and must be written to disk

questions.explanation: The 'i' (invalid) bit indicates that the page is either not in the process's logical address space or is on disk rather than in RAM.

questions.text: Which steps are involved in handling a page fault? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Trap to the operating system
choices.text: Trap to the operating system

choices.label: Find a free frame
choices.text: Find a free frame

choices.label: Swap the page into the frame via a disk operation
choices.text: Swap the page into the frame via a disk operation

choices.label: Set the validation bit to 'v' and restart the instruction
choices.text: Set the validation bit to 'v' and restart the instruction

choices.label: Abort the process immediately
choices.text: Abort the process immediately

questions.explanation: If the reference is valid but the page is not in memory, the OS finds a frame, loads the page, updates the table, and restarts the instruction.

questions.text: What is 'pure demand paging'?

choices.label: Loading the first 10 pages of a process before execution
choices.text: Loading the first 10 pages of a process before execution

choices.label: Starting a process with zero pages in memory and faulting for every page
choices.text: Starting a process with zero pages in memory and faulting for every page

choices.label: A system where pages are never swapped out to disk
choices.text: A system where pages are never swapped out to disk

choices.label: A system that uses segmentation instead of paging
choices.text: A system that uses segmentation instead of paging

questions.explanation: Pure demand paging starts a process with no pages in memory, relying entirely on page faults to bring in required code and data.

questions.text: Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT) for a system with a memory access time of 200ns, a page-fault service time of 8ms, and a page fault rate \( p \).
questions.explanation: The formula for EAT in demand paging is (1 - page fault rate) * memory access time + (page fault rate) * page fault service time.

questions.text: How does the 'Copy-on-Write' (COW) mechanism optimize process creation?

choices.label: It copies all pages of the parent process to the child process immediately
choices.text: It copies all pages of the parent process to the child process immediately

choices.label: It allows parent and child to share pages until one of them modifies a page
choices.text: It allows parent and child to share pages until one of them modifies a page

choices.label: It eliminates the need for a page table
choices.text: It eliminates the need for a page table

choices.label: It prevents the child process from accessing any memory
choices.text: It prevents the child process from accessing any memory

questions.explanation: COW allows for efficient `fork()` by sharing pages. A copy is only made when a process attempts to write to a shared page.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the 'modify bit' (dirty bit) in page replacement?

choices.label: To indicate if a page is read-only
choices.text: To indicate if a page is read-only

choices.label: To reduce overhead by only writing modified pages back to disk
choices.text: To reduce overhead by only writing modified pages back to disk

choices.label: To track the total number of times a page was accessed
choices.text: To track the total number of times a page was accessed

choices.label: To implement Belady's Anomaly
choices.text: To implement Belady's Anomaly

questions.explanation: The modify bit tracks whether a page has been changed. If it hasn't (it is 'clean'), it doesn't need to be written back to disk when replaced.

questions.text: Which of the following describes 'Belady’s Anomaly'?

choices.label: As memory access speed increases, page faults increase
choices.text: As memory access speed increases, page faults increase

choices.label: For some page-replacement algorithms, the page-fault rate may increase as the number of allocated frames increases
choices.text: For some page-replacement algorithms, the page-fault rate may increase as the number of allocated frames increases

choices.label: The optimal algorithm always produces the most page faults
choices.text: The optimal algorithm always produces the most page faults

choices.label: Allocating more frames always results in fewer page faults
choices.text: Allocating more frames always results in fewer page faults

questions.explanation: Belady's Anomaly is a counter-intuitive phenomenon specifically seen in FIFO page replacement where more frames can lead to more faults.

questions.text: In the Optimal (OPT) page replacement algorithm, which page is selected for replacement?

choices.label: The page that has been in memory the longest
choices.text: The page that has been in memory the longest

choices.label: The page that will not be used for the longest period of time in the future
choices.text: The page that will not be used for the longest period of time in the future

choices.label: The page that was used most recently
choices.text: The page that was used most recently

choices.label: The page with the smallest counter
choices.text: The page with the smallest counter

questions.explanation: The Optimal algorithm replaces the page that will not be needed for the longest future duration, providing the lowest possible page fault rate.

questions.text: Which of these are methods for implementing Least Recently Used (LRU) replacement? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Counter implementation (Time-of-use)
choices.text: Counter implementation (Time-of-use)

choices.label: Stack implementation (Double-linked list)
choices.text: Stack implementation (Double-linked list)

choices.label: Purely random selection
choices.text: Purely random selection

choices.label: Reference bit approximation
choices.text: Reference bit approximation

questions.explanation: LRU can be implemented exactly using counters or stacks, or approximated using reference bits in hardware.

questions.text: How does the 'Second-Chance' (Clock) algorithm differ from standard FIFO?

choices.label: It replaces pages based on their size
choices.text: It replaces pages based on their size

choices.label: It uses a reference bit to give a page another chance if it was recently used
choices.text: It uses a reference bit to give a page another chance if it was recently used

choices.label: It replaces the most recently used page
choices.text: It replaces the most recently used page

choices.label: It requires knowing future memory references
choices.text: It requires knowing future memory references

questions.explanation: Second-chance uses a reference bit. If the bit is 1, it's cleared and the page is moved to the end of the queue rather than being evicted.

questions.text: The 'Enhanced Second-Chance' algorithm uses an ordered pair (reference, modify). Which class is the best candidate for replacement?

choices.label: (1, 1): Recently used and modified
choices.text: (1, 1): Recently used and modified

choices.label: (1, 0): Recently used but clean
choices.text: (1, 0): Recently used but clean

choices.label: (0, 1): Not recently used but modified
choices.text: (0, 1): Not recently used but modified

choices.label: (0, 0): Neither recently used nor modified
choices.text: (0, 0): Neither recently used nor modified

questions.explanation: (0,0) is the best candidate because it hasn't been used recently and doesn't need a disk write-back.

questions.text: What is 'Thrashing' in an operating system?

choices.label: When the CPU is executing at 100% capacity
choices.text: When the CPU is executing at 100% capacity

choices.label: When a process spends more time paging (swapping) than executing
choices.text: When a process spends more time paging (swapping) than executing

choices.label: When the system shuts down due to lack of memory
choices.text: When the system shuts down due to lack of memory

choices.label: When the disk head stops moving
choices.text: When the disk head stops moving

questions.explanation: Thrashing occurs when the total size of localities exceeds total physical memory, causing constant page faults and low CPU utilization.

questions.text: Which model is used to prevent thrashing by tracking the set of pages a process is currently using?

choices.label: FIFO Queue Model
choices.text: FIFO Queue Model

choices.label: Working-Set Model
choices.text: Working-Set Model

choices.label: Binary Search Model
choices.text: Binary Search Model

choices.label: Round Robin Model
choices.text: Round Robin Model

questions.explanation: The Working-Set Model defines a window of recent references to approximate locality and ensure the process has enough frames to avoid thrashing.

questions.text: In memory-mapped files, how is file I/O treated?

choices.label: As manual sector-by-sector disk writes
choices.text: As manual sector-by-sector disk writes

choices.label: As routine memory accesses (load/store) to a mapped page
choices.text: As routine memory accesses (load/store) to a mapped page

choices.label: As a sequence of `read()` and `write()` system calls only
choices.text: As a sequence of `read()` and `write()` system calls only

choices.label: As encrypted network packets
choices.text: As encrypted network packets

questions.explanation: Memory-mapped I/O maps a disk block to a page in RAM, allowing standard memory instructions to interact with file data.

questions.text: What are the two common strategies for allocating kernel memory? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Buddy System
choices.text: Buddy System

choices.label: Slab Allocation
choices.text: Slab Allocation

choices.label: FIFO Allocation
choices.text: FIFO Allocation

choices.label: Belady Allocation
choices.text: Belady Allocation

questions.explanation: The Buddy System (power-of-2) and Slab Allocation (caches for data structures) are the primary kernel memory management schemes.

questions.text: What is the primary advantage of the Buddy System allocator?

choices.label: It eliminates internal fragmentation entirely
choices.text: It eliminates internal fragmentation entirely

choices.label: It can quickly coalesce unused adjacent chunks into larger segments
choices.text: It can quickly coalesce unused adjacent chunks into larger segments

choices.label: It uses a circular queue to manage pages
choices.text: It uses a circular queue to manage pages

choices.label: It only works with non-contiguous memory
choices.text: It only works with non-contiguous memory

questions.explanation: The Buddy System allows 'buddies' to be merged back into larger power-of-2 blocks quickly when they are freed.

questions.text: How does 'Slab Allocation' minimize fragmentation?

choices.label: By using power-of-2 chunks for every request
choices.text: By using power-of-2 chunks for every request

choices.label: By pre-allocating caches for specific kernel data structures (like `task_struct`)
choices.text: By pre-allocating caches for specific kernel data structures (like `task_struct`)

choices.label: By swapping kernel code to disk
choices.text: By swapping kernel code to disk

choices.label: By increasing the page size to 4MB
choices.text: By increasing the page size to 4MB

questions.explanation: Slab allocation uses caches tailored to the size of specific kernel objects, ensuring that memory is used efficiently without splitting pages into awkward sizes.

questions.text: What is 'TLB Reach'?

choices.label: The maximum number of pages in the swap file
choices.text: The maximum number of pages in the swap file

choices.label: The amount of memory accessible from the Translation Lookaside Buffer
choices.text: The amount of memory accessible from the Translation Lookaside Buffer

choices.label: The time it takes to restart an instruction
choices.text: The time it takes to restart an instruction

choices.label: The physical distance between the CPU and RAM
choices.text: The physical distance between the CPU and RAM

questions.explanation: TLB Reach = (TLB Size) * (Page Size). It represents how much memory the CPU can address without incurring a page table walk.

questions.text: Why does Program 1 (looping by column then row) cause more page faults than Program 2 (looping by row then column) in a row-major array storage system?

choices.label: Program 1 uses more variables
choices.text: Program 1 uses more variables

choices.label: Program 1 violates locality of reference by jumping between pages on every access
choices.text: Program 1 violates locality of reference by jumping between pages on every access

choices.label: Program 2 is written in a higher-level language
choices.text: Program 2 is written in a higher-level language

choices.label: The operating system gives Program 2 more frames
choices.text: The operating system gives Program 2 more frames

questions.explanation: In row-major storage, rows are contiguous. Accessing column-wise means jumping across pages repeatedly, causing a fault for nearly every access.

questions.text: In the Solaris operating system, what happens when free memory reaches the 'Minfree' threshold?

choices.label: The system starts demand paging
choices.text: The system starts demand paging

choices.label: The system begins swapping entire processes
choices.text: The system begins swapping entire processes

choices.label: The system increases the scan rate of pages
choices.text: The system increases the scan rate of pages

choices.label: The system turns off the MMU
choices.text: The system turns off the MMU

questions.explanation: Solaris uses thresholds: 'Lotsfree' starts paging, but 'Minfree' indicates critical memory pressure, triggering swapping of whole processes.

questions.text: What is 'Working Set Trimming' in Windows?

choices.label: Deleting files from the hard drive
choices.text: Deleting files from the hard drive

choices.label: Removing pages from processes that have pages in excess of their working set minimum when free memory is low
choices.text: Removing pages from processes that have pages in excess of their working set minimum when free memory is low

choices.label: Reducing the priority of background threads
choices.text: Reducing the priority of background threads

choices.label: Increasing the size of the page file
choices.text: Increasing the size of the page file

questions.explanation: Windows restores free memory by taking frames away from processes that have exceeded their guaranteed minimum allocation.
